data.title: indie Semiconductor's Pivot To Higher Margins: The emotion3D Acquisition
data.description:
The recent acquisition of Vienna-based AI perception software developer emotion3d is a pivotal moment in the lifecycle of indie Semiconductor, Inc. (NASDAQ:INDI). Until now, INDI was a pureplay developer of hardware (with related minor software) for automotive semiconductor systems. This was a momentum story given the strong growth potential of the automotive automation market; however, so far, INDI was only focusing on half the story. Doubtless, at ~$149 billion TAM, automation hardware was a big market; but that left out the ~$90 billion TAM automation software market for INDI, while its larger rivals diversified broadly. With this acquisition, INDI is now poised to quickly enter this higher-margin growth area.

Overview of indie

indie is a fabless designer of automotive semiconductor systems like ADAS or Advanced Driver Assistance Systems. The company broadly described itself as “developing advanced silicon, photonics, and sensor system solutions demanded by the automotive, industrial mobility, and adjacent applications.” The company’s broad portfolio of radar + vision + photonics + in-cabin electronics lets them access a total addressable market or TAM of $149 billion. This is a high-growth market with ~10+% CAGR opportunities as momentum grows in the automation market. As the company notes, theirs is the “pursuit of the uncrashable vehicle.”

In the next 5 years, INDI estimates a 6x growth in their solutions adding to vehicle silicone content:

They expect to ramp up SoC shipments to most of the major automobile companies of the world to the tune of 500+ million in 2025, up from 400 million last year. As of last year, their strategic design-win backlog was a staggering $7.1 billion, 72% of which was from ADAS.

The company’s recent purchase of emotion3D with its AI perception software suite lets them immediately create an accretive and high margin revenue stream through licensing royalties. INDI can bundle their vision and radar SoCs with emotion3d’s in-cabin (driver/occupant monitoring) and external sensing (camera-radar fusion, night vision) to create a synergistic software-hardware ensemble. First, software is a high-margin business, second, per McKinsey, automotive software will become a $83 billion market by 2030 (and $135 billion for automotive semiconductors ), and third, this is INDI’s first real foray into automotive software - so the acquisition takes on a lot of importance for the future of the company.

The emotion3d acquisition: rationale and synergies

indie has a track record of strategic growth through acquisition. For example, between 2021 and 2023, indie acquired companies like TeraXion (2021, $159 million) and EXALOS (2023, $65 million) for their LiDAR and photonics components. These two acquisitions positioned it to offer complete photonic sub-systems for LiDAR and heads-up displays.

The August 2025 acquisition of emotion3D comes with modest deal terms - $20 million in upfront cash and another $10 million performance contingencies. Compared to the two earlier buys, this one is small, but it could be transformative for the company, signaling its first foray into automotive software. While small, emotion3D has multiple tier 1 and OEM automotive clients, and has been described as a “leader” in the perception software space.

This acquisition is a major step towards making indie a full stack offer, complementing its silicon with emotion3D’s AI-driven software for camera/radar data interpretation. Such a strategy is similar to what Mobileye (MBLY) and Qualcomm (QCOM), indie’s larger rivals, adopt, combining silicon with perception software. Unlike pure hardware sales (~50% margins are standard), software can be both higher margin (~90% margins are not uncommon) and recurring or license-oriented, which will improve indie’s gross margins.

I have already mentioned the much larger TAM that will open up for indie through this acquisition. I should note that the company tells us that “ADAS and automated driving software will comprise over 50 percent of this total, marking a significant incremental revenue opportunity for automotive semiconductors.” This helps it gain new customers as well as open up new revenue streams from existing customers who prefer a one-stop type shop for these services.

Emotion3D already has experience partnering with major companies, like Ambarella (AMBA) and onsemi. With Ambarella – a leading vision SoC supplier – emotion3D collaborated to optimize driver and occupant monitoring software on Ambarella’s CV series processors. With onsemi, a top image sensor maker, Emotion3D worked on reference designs for DMS/OMS cameras. Also, as showcased at CES 2023, Emotion3D co-developed an intelligent restraint system with Tier-1 Veoneer. These varied collaborations highlight the hardware-agnostic approach of Emotion3D, which will now be accretive to indie. There will also be opportunities to cross-sell; so indie can sell its chips to Emotion3D’s customers, as well as bundle Emotion3D software to its own chip customers.

Emotion3D’s software does both in-cabin monitoring and exterior ADAS sensing. The in-cabin part monitors driver facial features, eye gaze, and occupant status in real time via cameras and can detect if the driver is becoming drowsy or distracted and whether occupants have seat belts on and so on. The software algorithm conforms to advanced regulations like EU General Safety Regulation, Euro NCAP protocols etc. In the exterior sensing department, the software can offer enhanced nighttime vision and camera-radar sensing fusion. These tasks can be handled in a low-compute environment suitable for automotive-grade processors. Other ways to reduce costs include the Emotion3D team’s expertise in synthetic data generation and simulation to train AI models. This reduces the cost of real-world data generation, which is a costly component of training.

Market Expansion and New Opportunities Post-Acquisition

emotion3D’s capabilities immediately help expand indie’s market into newer areas. One of these is DMS/OMS, or Driver/Occupant Monitoring Systems. indie’s hardware (camera + IR + sensors) can be directly integrated with emotion3D’s software to provide a complete in-cabin monitoring solution to vehicle manufacturers. New regulations like the European GSR open up these markets by regulating that new vehicles sold in the EU must include drowsiness and distraction detection systems. According to GSR, all new vehicles sold in the EU must have Driver Drowsiness and Attention Warning (MID-2024), and by 2026, an Advanced Driver Distraction Warning (camera-based) system will be mandatory in all new cars.

These European regulations are widely emulated in other countries. As emotion3D tells us:

Therefore, it is safe to say that the protocols and roadmaps of Euro NCAP carry importance for the global automotive scene.

As technology continues to advance and these systems become more refined, we can expect even greater improvements in road safety. emotion3D is at the forefront of innovation when it comes to all things in-cabin analysis. Our technology covers all current regulations and NCAPs

In the US as well, NHTSA is studying ways to reduce driver distraction, and various US automakers are actively implementing these DMS systems. This is fast expanding the TAM for OMS/DMS, and companies that specialize in this field - Seeing Machines (OTCPK:SEEMF), Smart Eye (OTCPK:SMTEF) - are seeing traction. With the acquisition of emotion3D, indie can now directly tap into this market, with the added advantage of offering both hardware and software.

Outside the cabin, emotion3D has algorithms for external ADAS perception that can create opportunities for indie in forward collision avoidance, pedestrian detection, and sensor fusion systems. emotion3D’s camera-radar sensor fusion is a case in point. It combines camera vision with radar and LiDAR data for better object detection, tools that are highly useful for advanced ADAS and semiautonomous driving. Indie, which already produces radar sensor chips and LiDAR components, can now offer the perception software to fuse those sensors’ data. This sort of vertical integration improves indie’s competitiveness, making it more attractive to Tier 1 manufacturers looking for complete solutions. In areas like low-light pedestrian detection too - part of emotion3D’s nighttime vision solutions - the indie+emotion3D combo can offer vertically integrated products. As the industry gradually moves toward more automated driving (over a 5+ year horizon), Indie’s combined hardware/software offering could capture a slice of that market, competing (on a smaller scale) with players like Mobileye’s SuperVision or Qualcomm’s Arriver platform in specific niches (e.g. radar-centric ADAS). All of this goes towards increasing value per vehicle and cross-selling opportunities.

Competitive Landscape – How Indie Stacks Up vs. Larger Peers

Indie used to be a hardware-only player, but this new acquisition now pits it directly against larger, vertically integrated players. There are opportunities for both competition and partnership, given some of the larger players like Ambarella were already collaborating with emotion3D for its software.

Ambarella at around $3.65 billion market cap is a mid-sized chip manufacturer. Starting out as a video processing chipmaker for cameras and drones, it now offers computer vision SoCs for automotive industries worldwide. Ambarella’s CVflow family of AI processors targets ADAS cameras, and the company, like Indie, has augmented its silicon with software acquisitions (e.g. the purchase of Oculii for radar perception algorithms in 2021). Ambarella also partners with independent software makers - like with emotion3D itself - to offer DMS solutions on top of its chips. Ambarella’s revenue ($285 million in 2024) is higher than indie’s ($217 million in 2024), but valuation-wise, Ambarella at 10x EV/revenue is less attractive than indie at 5x. Both are unprofitable growth plays, and both are trying to improve margins through vertical integration. Since Ambarella started a few years early, its gross margins (58-60%) are still higher than indie’s 50%. Historically, AMBA was better at high-performance imaging chips, while indie’s strength was mixed-signal integration and now photonic sensors. Given Ambarella’s focus on camera-based systems, Indie’s inclusion of radar, LiDAR, and ultrasonic domains could allow it to differentiate via a more holistic sensor suite, basically punching above its weight and attracting automotive manufacturers who often look for multiple sources of supply.

Mobileye is the behemoth in automotive vision/ADAS, with 80% of the market share in Level 1/2 ADAS vision systems and 2024 revenue of $1.65 billion. MBLY offers the largely adopted EyeQ system-on-chips along with its full software stack (for lane keeping, automatic emergency braking, adaptive cruise, etc.) for a complete ADAS solution. While much larger, Mobileye has more capabilities in camera vision and front-facing ADAS (camera-based), while indie has capabilities in multi sensor components and algorithms for in-cabin and other applications. Manufacturers generally use Mobileye for front-facing ADAS and another vendor for driver monitoring, opening an opportunity for the likes of indie.

Valuation-wise, MBLY has adjusted gross margins of ~67%, reflecting its software-heavy focus. It trades at around 6-7x sales, which is strong for such a large company. indie should replicate Mobileye’s playbook, improving margins as it focuses more on software using the emotion3D acquisition, offering more vertically integrated products in areas where Mobileye is not a major player.

Larger players like Qualcomm and Nvidia (NVDA) have entered the automotive sector with powerful SoCs. Some of them offer full stack capabilities, with Qualcomm acquiring Arriver’s ADAS software from Veoneer in 2022. These players offer a co-option scenario for the likes of indie; for example, an OEM using Qualcomm’s platform might still need Indie’s LiDAR lasers or its ultrasonic chips. indie can do well in a cost-sensitive, specialized situation where going with larger players may not make sense.

Large OEMs like Bosch and Denso often have their own software products. Here, indie’s smaller size may actually offer advantages in the form of better customization, lower costs and flexibility. There are also pure software plays like Seeing Machines (Australia) and Smart Eye (Sweden), two leading DMS algo suppliers. The competition is, thus, intense, but indie has advantages at multiple levels, which it can harness to capture larger market shares, both horizontally and vertically.

Financials, Valuation, and Outlook

Current financial snapshot and development highlights: In Q2 2025, indie had $51.6 million in revenue, down 1.4% Y/Y but above guidance midpoint ($51 M – $52 M); driven by radar and vision programs entering early production. Non-GAAP Gross Margin of 49.1% was stable quarter over quarter. It is expected to reach 49 – 50% in Q3 as product mix improves with ADAS ramps. Following the 2024 restructuring, non-GAAP OpEx has reached $39.9 million, and the company expects this to go down further with $8-10 million in quarterly savings. Total cash, including restricted cash, was $202.9 million. Cash burn fell sharply from $25 million in Q1 to $10 million in Q2, and is expected to stabilize at around $7.5 million on average over the next two quarters, helping the company achieve breakeven OpEx. The company repurchased $30 million worth of 2027 convertibles at a discount, saving on interest and improving EPS. They are also exploring a sale or IPO of their 34% stake in WuXi indie in order to free up cash and focus on ADAS.

Other developments include their flagship 77 GHz radar chipset, which is nearing production with Tier 1 customers. Their Vision portfolio (iND880 ISP/processor) is also gaining traction with Chinese OEMs for camera and occupancy monitoring systems, with plans for design-to-production in < 12 months.

Valuation relative to peers: indie’s EV/sales is much lower than its larger rivals like Ambarella and Mobileye. This discount reflects execution risks and the company’s early stage. Looking at it another way, there is also upside potential if the company can execute properly. Even a move to 6x sales (still below Ambarella) at, say, $300 M revenue in a few years would imply an ~$1.8 B EV, over double today’s level.

If we look at the strategic backlog, as well, that is nearly 6x its market cap as of today. While a design win is not guaranteed revenue, it does indicate the value of the company’s programs. If some of those convert to actual sales, I see a lot of upside potential.

Risks and Challenges

While I like the long-term outlook based on the emotion3D acquisition, integration is an execution challenge investors should be well aware of. Management has to allot bandwidth for retaining talent, engaging existing customers and acquiring new ones, all the while retaining bandwidth on its ADAS hardware vertical.

The acquisition also opens indie up to greater and more intense competition from larger players well-entrenched with software+hardware products. These larger companies have advantages of scale that indie will be competing against. Moreover, its closed stack nature may not always appeal to customers comfortable with sourcing hardware and software from different vendors.

In the short to midterm, tariffs may have an impact on indie’s business. Although the company assures us that it has geographical bundling in manufacturing and customers, so that tariff impact is minimal, this is not always true in certain markets. With the tariff war intensifying, the situation remains fluid and uncertain.

Investment Summary and Conclusion

indie offers a differentiated investment opportunity in a growing niche, and its early-stage nature implies there is still considerable upside. This new software acquisition takes it to an inflection point from where there is a good chance that it will grow further, seeing higher margins and improved earnings. The bull-case rests on execution, so if you are confident in management’s ability to do so, this is a buy at dips.

data.title: Eaton: My Favorite 2nd Derivative AI Investment Is A Buy
data.description:
My followers know that my primary AI investments are Google (GOOG), Broadcom (AVGO), Amazon (AMZN), and the VanEck Semiconductors ETF (SMH). My first derivative AI choice is U.S. utility companies - specifically NextEra (NEE) and the SPDR Select Sector Utility ETF (XLU). I cover and have written about all these companies and funds on Seeking Alpha but today I will return to my favorite second derivative AI company: Eaton Corp. (NYSE:ETN). I call it a second derivative AI play because Eaton is a major electrical equipment supplier to my first derivative play: utility companies (both in the U.S. and abroad). That being the case, the current AI data-center build-out is a very positive catalyst for Eaton moving forward, as is the fact that since my last Seeking Alpha article on Eaton, it has completed a major expansion project in the United States that is going to increase Eaton's revenue in the coming quarters. Eaton's Q3 earnings are due out before the market opens on November 4th. Today, I will review the investment thesis in Eaton and offer you a Q3 earnings preview.

Investment Thesis

Back in January of last year, I wrote my first Seeking Alpha article on Eaton (see Eaton: Bullish On This Electrical Equipment Superpower) and initiated my coverage on the company with a BUY recommendation. I was bullish on Eaton for the simple reason that it seemed to me that the U.S. would need to add substantial electrical power generation capacity for the electrification, EV, and - specifically - for the AI data-center build-out themes. A recovery in Eaton's commercial and military aerospace businesses and opportunities for continued margin expansion were also primary catalysts. That was a good call: as you can see from the graphic below, since that article was published Eaton has outperformed both the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq-100 as represented by the (VOO) and (QQQ) ETFs:

However, note that my first derivative AI play - the XLU Utilities ETF - actually took the top-spot in this comparison. The XLU ETF was one of my choices in The 4-Fund Trump Portfolio, a Seeking Alpha Editor's Pick, back in early May.

Eaton holds ~30% of the global market share for electrical equipment, and I give it a wide moat around its Electrical Americas segment. This market is arguably an oligarchy dominated by Eaton, Siemens, GE Vernova (GEV), and Square D - Schneider Electric's (OTCPK:SBGSF)(OTCPK:SBGSY) U.S. brand.

Today, the AI/Electrification investment thesis is not only still firmly in place, but to that we can now add three of Eaton's recent growth initiatives: production capacity expansion in the United States, strategic partnerships, and growth via M&A. Today, I'll cover all three and then get to the Q3 earnings preview.

Capacity Expansion

Earlier this month, Eaton announced completion of its $100 million expansion project in Texas that added 200,000 sq. ft and more than doubled the company's domestic production capacity of voltage regulators and three-phase transformers. Eaton is one of the world's largest producers of voltage regulators, and the company is seeing strong demand for them due to one of the strategic partnerships that Eaton CEO Paulo Ruiz Sternadt described during the Q2 conference call with the aid of the slide below from the Q2 presentation:

One pillar of Eaton's growth strategy is to invest in high-growth, high-margin markets, and the slide above (slide 7 of the Q2 presentation) summarizes Eaton's "growth through strategic partnerships" plan. CEO Sternadt said:

You see 3 examples here on Page 7, NVIDIA, Siemens Energy and ChargePoint. We partnered with NVIDIA to transform the infrastructure of data centers. NVIDIA understands the design is chip out and they recognize we are the partner they need. We bring incredible expertise and capabilities in power distribution architecture, including higher voltage DC power. So we are developing power management solutions for their high-density GPUs and solving other problems in the rack.

From this comment, and other industry analysis, it's clear that NVIDIA (NVDA) is moving to a higher voltage DC (HVDC) powered architecture for its data center racks. That being the case, it is no surprise that earlier this month Eaton unveiled its next-generation 800V DC power architecture for "AI factories" and said:

Eaton’s new design, built in support of the 800 VDC architecture announced by NVIDIA, leverages Eaton’s innovative power management technology to protect and optimize high-density computing infrastructure and serves as a critical milestone in Eaton’s grid-to-chip strategy for meeting the increasing energy demands of AI data centers ... NVIDIA is working with leading power, silicon and data center partners to pioneer the adoption of 800 VDC and help unlock new levels of scalability and energy optimization in AI factories.

Where NVIDIA leads, many others are likely to follow if they're not already there (I am thinking of hyperscalers like Google, AWS, and Microsoft). This was a primary reason behind Eaton's decision to double the production capacity of high-voltage regulators at the Texas plant and is a bullish catalyst moving forward. Indeed, on the previously referenced conference call, CEO Sternady said that growth will be led by the "3 biggest" drivers for the second half of this year: transformers, switching gear, and voltage regulators.

The Siemens Energy (OTCPK:SIEGY)(OTCPK:SMAWF)(OTCPK:SMEGF), partnership is another excellent move by Eaton's management. I say that because this partnership steps in when utilities can't provide enough power to data center operators. Here, Siemens will handle the on-site power generation, and Eaton is in charge of the modular power distribution system. Together, Eaton & Siemens will deliver flexible distributed power for data centers that have no dependency on the grid. This means shorter project build-times and greater operational flexibility for Eaton's customers.

Last quarter, the AI data-center build-out drove Eaton's financial returns:

The Electrical Americas is Eaton's largest and fastest growing segment (48% of Q2 total revenue and 16% growth), driven primarily by data center business. On the Q2 call, CEO Sternadt said:

And we continue to deliver robust growth in the data center market as well. Our orders jumped approximately 55% and our sales were 50% up versus Q2 2024.

Note that recent acquisitions added 5% sales growth to the Electrical Americas Segment as the Fibrebond deal was closed in Q2. Eaton paid $1.4 billion for Fibrebond, and it is expected to generate $110 million of adjusted EBITDA in 2025. A new agreement to acquire Resilient Power Systems was recently announced, and Eaton already closed that deal on August 6th. I do not have an estimate for adjusted EBITDA contributions for the Resilient deal.

Resilient Power Systems will help Eaton distribute power all the way from the utility company connection to the chips in the rack:

Or to the charger for your EV, which makes the Resilient acquisition symbiotic with the third strategic partnership listed on the first slide I presented above from the Q2 presentation, with ChargePoint (CHPT). The goal of that partnership is for Eaton and ChargePoint to jointly develop global integrated EV charging, power distribution and software solutions for EV electrification at scale.

Q3 Earnings Preview

During the Q2 conference call, Eaton presented its 2025 assumptions, which are summarized on the slide below:

As you can see, Eaton expects 32% of total 2025 revenue will come from very high growth end-markets: Data Centers, Commercial Aerospace, and Defense Aerospace - with, not surprisingly, data centers leading the charge. As you can also see, only 17% of total revenue exposure is expected from declining segments while Eaton's largest sub-segment (20% of revenue) is the Commercial & Institutional end-market, which is expected to grow - albeit just not as fast as the top categories. Regardless, my point here is that the big picture for Eaton is bullish as most of the company's biggest end-markets are growing fast. In addition, it is a diversified set of end-markets - and that lowers risks.

Due to the bullish outlook, Eaton raised guidance on the Q2 conference call for both organic growth (by 50 basis points) and operating margin (by 10 basis points):

As a result, adjusted EPS for FY2025 was raised to a midpoint of $12.07/share, which equates to a 12% YoY increase. The midpoint of Q3 adjusted EPS guidance was $3.04, which is a penny below current analysts consensus estimates for Q3 (courtesy of Yahoo.Finance):

For the full-year, note that the average current consensus estimate is for $12.09/share, which is above management's current midpoint of $12.07/share. I agree with this sentiment because it seems as though there has been nothing but bullish news in the AI data center market. Eaton also said on the Q2 conference call: "We see order acceleration, increase in negotiation pipeline, and strong growth in our backlog". So, in my opinion, Eaton will like post another beat-and-raise in Q3. As always, investors should keep an eye on margins and free-cash-flow. Operating margin in Q2 was 23.9%, +20 bps yoy. Free-cash-flow in Q2 was only $716 million., which means we should expect a significant increase in FCF in the Q3 report in order for Eaton to meet the midpoint of its FY25 FCF estimate of $3.9 billion (see 2025 Guidance graphic above).

Valuation

Eaton is yet another AI-related company that is trading at a lofty valuation premium as compared to the S&P500:

  • P/E = 37.4x
  • Forward P/E = 29.8x
  • Dividend/Yield = $4.16/1.15%

In addition, near-term free-cash-flow generation has been negatively impacted by tariffs and acquisition expenses. Still, from the 2025 Guidance slide above, the company - at the midpoint - expects to generate $3.9 billion of free-cash-flow this year. That equates to an estimated FCF = $9.96/share based on the 391.4 million average fully-diluted shares outstanding at the end of Q2.

That strong free-cash-flow profile is further strengthened by ETN's backlog, which, despite record sales in Q2, the book-to-bill ratio increased to 1.1x and that resulted in 17% growth in its large $11.4 billion backlog. That backlog not only equates to ~6-months worth of revenue going forward, but Eaton has stronger visibility as it expects the backlog will not decline in Q3. My point here is that there is a relatively clear near- and mid-term line of sight into strong revenue and free-cash-flow generation. However, my opinion is that we are still in the early innings of what I consider to be a long-term secular bull-market in AI, utilities, and therefore electrical equipment manufacturing (i.e. Eaton). That being the case, and while Eaton certainly trades at a premium valuation to the S&P 500, it's arguably rational because it is based on the assumption of multiple years of high single- to low-to-mid double-digit revenue growth combined with strong free-cash-flow generation and opportunities for additional margin expansion. And I believe that is exactly what Eaton will deliver.

Risks

As just mentioned, Eaton stock is highly valued, and the dividend yield of only 1.15% does not provide downside protection in my opinion.

A significant and prolonged reduction in AI data-center capacity expansion would pull the rug out from underneath Eaton's 37.4x multiple. For example, a contraction of the P/E to a more reasonable 25x would equate to a drop in the stock price from the close on Thursday of $372 to around $250, or -30%+.

Meantime, Eaton's vehicle and eMobility segments continue to underperform and are a drag on Eaton's overall growth rate, and that trend looks to continue over the near-term horizon.

The macro global environment still faces significant risks from President Trump's arguably erratic, chaotic, and very uncertain tariff war, which could significantly slow U.S. and global economic growth and possibly lead to a recession. Indeed, some economists believe the U.S. would already be in a recession were it not for job creation driven by the current AI boom in compute capacity expansion.

Summary & Conclusion

I like Eaton, and I think it's a stock investors should own over the next 5-years (at least). The company has been making smart partnerships with leading companies; it is focused on high-growth, high-margin businesses; and it is expanding production capacity to meet strong demand in its core businesses - like voltage regulators, transformers, and switching equipment. As a result, Eaton still has opportunities for continued margin expansion across its portfolio. The company has a strong balance sheet (only 1.8x leverage), has a very strong free-cash-flow profile, and management typically under-promises and over-delivers (much better than the other way around). I rate ETN a BUY.

That said, ETN stock is quite volatile. Indeed, just this week it traded down, and then up, more than $10/share:

That being the case, and similar to my BUY advice on most all of the biggest and best AI plays, I suggest investors who don't yet own ETN stock buy a "starter position" - even if it's only 5 or 10 shares. Then track the stock, the earnings reports, and listen to the conference calls. Over time, you will get to know the company well and will be able to take advantage of market volatility to add to your initial position until you reach your ultimate allocation goal. For example, I took advantage of a 15% pullback in Eaton stock after last year's Q2 report (see Eaton: AI/Electrification Catalysts Persists - Buy The Dip) and again during President Trump's "Liberation Day" sell-off, to complete the establishment of a full position of ETN in my own personal portfolio. It is a position I will likely hold for years to come - or at least until the fundamentals of the company materially changes.

Specifically, and taking a look at the stock's one-year price chart below, I would nibble on more shares at $350, take a larger bite at $325, and eat the whole pie should you get an opportunity under $310:

My twelve-month price target on Eaton is $425 a share, or +14%. Longer-term, I expect Eaton's growth rate to accelerate a few percentage points as the key partnerships with NVIDIA, Siemens, and ChargePoint begin to kick into high-gear.

I'll end with a 5-year total returns chart that demonstrates just how successful Eaton's management has been in delivering excellent returns for ETN shareholders:

As you can see from the graphic, Eaton has more than doubled the returns of both the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq-100 (as represented by the VOO and QQQ ETFs, respectively). While I don't expect quite that level of "beat" going forward, I do think ETN could certainly continue to deliver total returns greater than that of the S&P500.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cisco's Valuation Has Caught Up To Reality
data.description:
It has been more than two years since I published my last article about Cisco Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:CSCO). I have rated Cisco as a “Buy” in several articles in the last few years – including my previous article. In the conclusion of my last article, I wrote:

Cisco is still a solid buy in my opinion and when calculating with moderate growth assumptions, the stock is still undervalued. And only time will tell if Splunk was a great acquisition (if the deal is not blocked in any way). But with the company’s solid balance sheet and no additional debt necessary I am not worried about the acquisition. And Splunk could be a great fit for Cisco and probably has the able to grow with a high pace justifying the 30 times free cash flow multiple for the business.

And my “Buy” rating was probably not false. Since my last article was published, Cisco’s stock price increased about 33%, and when including dividends, the total return was about 41.5%. This is without much doubt a solid performance over the last two years, but the S&P 500 increased 55.5% and therefore outperformed Cisco in the last two years.

In the following article I will argue that Cisco is not extremely undervalued anymore. While the stock price increased in the last two years, the fundamental business is still lagging, making the stock more overvalued again and making us question whether Cisco is still a “Buy” at this point.

Is Cisco Still Undervalued?

When trying to answer the question of whether Cisco is still undervalued, we can look at the simple valuation multiples. In late 2023, Cisco was trading for rather low valuation multiples, but in the meantime, the P/E ratio, as well as the P/FCF ratio, expanded, and at the time of writing, Cisco is trading for 27.2 times earnings and for 21.4 times free cash flow.

Cisco is clearly trading above its 5-year average for both valuation multiples. In the last five years, the average P/E ratio was 19.80, and the average P/FCF ratio was 15.82. Of course, we can make the point that the overall stock market is also trading for rather high valuation multiples, and countless companies are trading for even higher valuation multiples. But first, I think many stocks in the United States are clearly overvalued right now, and we should not take the high valuation multiples as a basis for a reasonable comparison. Second, different companies are growing at different rates, and I don’t know if we should assume high-growth rates for Cisco as well.

Quarterly Results

In mid-August 2025, Cisco reported fourth-quarter and full-year results for fiscal 2025. And while the company did slightly exceed expectations for earnings per share as well as revenue, I would describe the results as rather mixed. Revenue increased from $13,642 million in Q4 '24 to $14,673 million in Q3 '25, resulting in 7.6% year-over-year growth. Not only did revenue increase at a solid pace, but operating income also increased 31.5% year-over-year from $2,618 million in Q4 '24 to $3,442 million in Q4 '25. Diluted net income per share increased from $0.54 in Q4 '24 to $0.71 in Q4 '25 – resulting also in 31.5% year-over-year growth. And finally, diluted net income per share increased 13.8% year-over-year from $0.87 in the same quarter last year to $0.99 in this quarter.

Without much doubt, fourth quarter results were great, but one quarter is only a snapshot in time, and we should put these results in context. And when looking at the full-year results, the picture is less impressive. In fiscal 2025, total revenue increased 5.3% year-over-year from $53,803 million in the previous year to $56,654 million in this year. While revenue increased in the mid-single digits, operating income declined 0.5% year-over-year from $12,181 million in fiscal 2024 to $12,115 million in fiscal 2025. Diluted net income per share increased slightly (2.8% year-over-year) from $2.54 in fiscal 2024 to $2.61 in fiscal 2025. Adjusted, diluted net income per share increased from $3.73 in fiscal 2024 to $3.81 in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 2.1% year-over-year growth.

Guidance For Fiscal 2026

Now we might argue that Cisco is accelerating growth rates, and the fourth quarter might be more representative than the full-year results. However, when looking at the guidance for fiscal 2026, I don’t know if we should actually bet on Cisco’s growth accelerating.

Management is expecting revenue to be in a range between $59.0 billion and $60.0 billion, which is resulting in 4% to 6% growth for the top line. And GAAP earnings per share are expected to be between $2.79 on the lower end and $2.91 on the upper end. This is resulting either in a 1% decline or 3.5% year-over-year growth – depending on which end of the range we are looking at. Non-GAAP earnings per share are expected to be between $4.00 and $4.06 – this is resulting in 5% to 6.5% year-over-year growth.

This is solid guidance for Cisco, but at this point we should rather assume growth rates in the low-to-mid single digits and not double-digit growth rates.

Long-Term Growth

In fiscal 2026, management is expecting growth rates in the low-to-mid single digits, but when looking at analysts’ assumptions for the next few years, we see more optimism and higher growth rates. Between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2030, analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 7.89% during these five years.

Comparing these numbers to the growth rates in the last few years, we can be optimistic about Cisco actually accelerating growth. In the last ten years, revenue increased only with a CAGR of 1.43%, and earnings per share increased with a CAGR of 3.84%. Looking at the last five or last three years does not really improve the situation.

The decisive question right now is the following: Is Cisco able to accelerate growth in the years to come, or should we rather assume that the company will remain a low-growing behemoth? When talking about growth, several (technology) companies are mentioning AI as a catalyst, and Cisco is no exception.

During Deutsche Bank’s 2025 Technology Conference, the chief product officer, Jeetendra Patel, outlined the vision for the ideal enterprise AI network over the next five years:

I think what you'll see is -- firstly, we do a pretty meaningful amount of work with 4 key constituents of customers, and that really helps us out. So for example, we serve some of the largest hyperscalers. And as you folks might have tuned into our earnings call, you saw that we actually exceeded our target number for the year, and it was over a couple of billion dollars in orders. And so that's the first constituency.

The second one is Neocloud, third one is service providers. And the fact that we serve those three really helps us take all of the innovation that happens over there and take it to the enterprise. And so you could start to see a pretty kind of advantageous position where the learnings that we get from the hyperscalers who are the most sophisticated of the sophisticated can then be taken to the enterprise as well.

And orders in the fourth quarter were about $800 million, bringing the total order volume for fiscal 2025 twice as high as originally targeted. Especially for AI training, infrastructure is expected to maintain its momentum with web-scale customers building AI LLM training clusters. Looking at the product orders in more detail, the public sector actually reported a decline of 6%, while enterprise reported 5% growth. However, service providers and cloud increased product orders by 49% year-over-year in the fourth quarter.

Overall, management was clearly optimistic about the usage of AI in the years to come and Cisco profiting from this trend. During the last earnings call, CEO Charles H. Robbins stated:

We continue to use GenAI and Agentic systems across our customer experience organization with things like services as code and AI agents for in-product support, renewals and adoption. Today, over 2/3 of support cases are touched by AI and automation, which increases the proportion of complex cases we can solve within one day. We're also seeing increased usage of Cisco's own proprietary AI application internally with more advanced use cases emerging across engineering, sales, operations and our people policy and purpose organization, resulting in meaningful productivity gains for our teams.

To summarize, we are seeing clear demand for our technology across customer markets in addition to expanded opportunities as we move towards Agentic AI. We are innovating faster than ever before, making AI foundational in our designs, fusing security deep into our networking products and providing operational simplicity for our customers. And our strong performance is fueling our capital allocation model, returning significant value to our shareholders while positioning our business for success in fiscal '26.

We could argue that Cisco is well positioned to take advantage of major trends – including the shift towards the cloud and the cybersecurity trend. However, these trends have already existed for several years, and Cisco was not really able to grow at a high pace in the past. And although the picture seems to improve for Cisco, I am still rather pessimistic about whether Cisco might be able to grow in the high single or double digits.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

We can’t really deny that Cisco has growth potential in the years to come, but the question remains whether Cisco is still a good investment. To answer that question, we once again use a discounted cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for the stock.

In a first step, let’s determine a WACC for Cisco. As the risk-free rate, we use the 10-year treasury yield (4.00%), and we also calculate with a market risk premium of 6.00%. Beta, amount of total debt, equity value, cost of equity, as well as the tax rate, are taken from the last reported numbers. Calculating with these assumptions, we get a WACC of 8.64%.

Additionally, we calculate with the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (3,992 million) and the free cash flow of the last four quarters ($13,290 million). The free cash flow of the last four quarters also seems like a reasonable assumption, as the free cash flow margin of the last four quarters is in line with the long-term average.

Now let’s be optimistic and assume 8% growth for the next ten years, which would be more or less in line with analysts’ assumptions, followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. This leads to an intrinsic value of $72.38 for the stock, and Cisco can be seen as fairly valued right now.

If we change the discount rate to 8.64% (according to our calculation), we get an intrinsic value of $94.93 for Cisco and could argue that the stock is still undervalued and probably has room to run. But like I said, I will use a 10% discount rate – not only to make different companies more comparable, but also due to the targeted annual return of at least 10%.

Conclusion

Cisco was certainly not an outperformer in the last few years, but investors could generate solid results from investing in Cisco. And in my opinion, the stock could go a little higher in the next few months and probably reach its all-time high of $82 once again (marked more than 25 years ago in March 2000).

However, I am still skeptical if Cisco can grow at a high pace once again, and to be fairly valued, we already must assume mid-to-high single digit growth rates in the next ten years for the company. Therefore, I would downgrade Cisco to “Hold” once again.

data.title: Robert Half: Another Weak Quarter, But It's Now Reflected In Shares (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Robert Half (NYSE:RHI) have been a very poor performer over the past year, losing over half of their value. A weakening labor market has significantly reduced demand for its placement and contract services. Plus, the company has been slow, in my view, to reduce expenses sufficiently to operate in a persistently lower demand environment. I have been consistently bearish on shares, reiterating Robert Half as a “sell” in August. Since then, the stock has dropped another 22%. With updated financials and shares so beaten up, now is a good time to revisit RHI, especially with shares below even my price target.

Margins have compressed significantly

In the company’s third quarter, Robert Half earned $0.43, which matched analyst expectations even as revenue declined by a further 8% to $1.35 billion. Management is seeing weak hiring activity, and few new projects are starting, which has been a fairly consistent theme. At the same time, staff utilization continues to go down, which has pressured operating margins. I continue to believe staff reduction is necessary, given there is unlikely to be a “V” shaped recovery in demand.

Gross profits fell by $67 million to $504 million, and SG&A was only down 4% to $490 million. Adjusted SG&A as a share of sales rose by 160 bps to 34.9%, and this continues to squeeze the bottom line. As a result, operating income was $61 million, or just a 4.5% margin. A capital-light business, like a recruiting firm, should run with higher margins, and the relatively small reductions in SG&A have contributed significantly to the poor share price.

Macro pressures have been significant

At the same time, revenue trends are poor. Looking at adjusted growth rates, there really are no positives to call out, and there is not much evidence the business is nearing a meaningful turn in results. Globally, finance and administrative roles are each down double digits for a third straight quarter. After turning positive, technology flipped back negative. Protiviti also reported its first year over year decline in six quarters, representing a marked deceleration. The business is not performing well, given a very difficult macro environment.

The leading reason for this is quite clear and largely outside of the company’s control: the jobs market is softening. Since the Fed first began raising interest rates, we have seen hiring activity steadily decline, and we have now been in a three-year downtrend. The Fed at first actively sought to reduce excess labor demand to cool inflation. Now, though, we are seeing hiring activity that is running substantially below pre-COVID levels, amid signs of labor market softness.

With inflation lower and the jobs market weaker, we are now seeing some Fed rate cuts. Ultimately, though, I do not see the Fed succeeding (or even trying, frankly) to create meaningful upward momentum in the jobs market. Instead, I see efforts focused on arresting the decline and stabilizing the jobs market near current levels. This can help to stabilize Robert Half’s business around current revenue levels, but stabilization will not enable a material recovery in results in my view, at least not on top-line revenue.

Weakness was widespread

Looking at segment results, talent solutions revenue fell by 11%. The US dropped 11% to $649 million, while international sales declined by 12% to $207 million. Gross margins were stable at 38.9%. Talent solutions earned $24 million, down from $38 million last year, with margins compressing 120 bps to a mere 2.8%. Within contract talent, finance and accounting is about 75% of its business and was down around 10% to $554 million. Technology is holding up the best, down less than 2% to $158 million, but as noted above, this still represents a deceleration.

Protiviti sales fell by 3% on an adjusted basis. Gross margins declined by 370 bps to 20.9%. While there is still a solid pipeline of deals, management is seeing new contracts being smaller in scope and shorter duration in nature. This mix reduces medium-term revenue visibility and is contributing to the margin pressure that we are seeing. Protiviti operating margins were down 260 bps to 7.5% as profits fell by nearly a third to $37 million. Falling so sharply below 10% is meaningfully disappointing.

Thus far, the company is not seeing much of an impact from AI, as disclosed on the earnings call, but if these tools do eventually replicate human work, they could reduce demand for labor and hurt RHI. This is especially true given the company’s focus on white collar and finance roles. I do not expect AI to lead to mass unemployment or starkly shift underlying business trends. On net though, it does pose a risk, albeit a modest one. It bears continued monitoring.

Balance sheet and dividend limit downside from here

Importantly, the company does maintain a strong balance sheet. Robert Half has $365 million of cash on its balance sheet, or about $3.50 per share. This is down over $200 million year to date due to buybacks and its dividend. Its dividend costs about $235 million, and shares yield about 8%. Management is committed to its dividend. However, its dividend reduces its capacity for buybacks as RHI does not believe in levering up its balance sheet. As a result, it spent only $20 million on buybacks during the quarter.

Its share count is down 3.3% from last year, but I expect buybacks to essentially be halted over the next year. The company is likely to underearn its dividend by about $100 million this year, and by $50-100 million next year. Now, given its net cash position, I expect the company to sustain its dividend, which it can for some time. I do not expect a dividend cut over the next 18-24, but the current dividend is likely to represent essentially all of its capital return.

In Q4, management is guiding to $1.245-$1.345 billion of sales, pointing to further sequential declines. Of course, hiring activity often tends to slow towards year-end given the Holidays. Adjusting for working days and currency, management views the guide as pointing to sequential growth. This guidance implies a 7% decline from last year, slightly better than the drop seen in Q3. Earnings are expected to be in the $0.25-$0.35 range, down sequentially as margins continue to face pressure.

In August, I saw RHI earning about $1.30-$1.55 this year, which drove my price target of $31-34. Based on guidance, RHI should earn $1.25-$1.35 this year, at the low end of my expectations. Given its strong balance share, I have viewed about 20x earnings as a reasonable trough multiple, given what I expect to be a slow recovery but my view that 2025 should represent the bottom in earnings. That points to a $25-26 price based on its earnings. With its net cash position, I see $28-29 as fair value, which is where the stock trades today.

As a result, having seen the stock decline more than 60% since my first sell recommendation, I think RHI shares have finally experienced enough pain that it is now too late to sell shares here. As such, I am upgrading the stock to a “hold.” I see no rush to buy with the stock only at fair value and a rebound that is likely to be slow. But the downside has occurred, and I expect shares to largely stabilize in this area while offering a dividend I view as secure for at least 18-24 months.

data.title: VUSB: Parking Cash With Low Risk
data.description:
Introduction

As in these last days, today we will analyze another investment vehicle that serves to park liquidity, the Vanguard Ultra-Short Bond ETF (BATS:VUSB). This vehicle is designed to transform the cash it collects into a stable income stream by investing in the shortest stretch of the interest rate curve. The reader can place it between a paid account and a traditional short-term bond fund, with a very short duration. One of its main objectives is that it aspires to pay a somewhat higher carry than that paid by the American Treasury bills, tightly controlling its own volatility and minimizing duration and credit risk.

The fund's portfolio is actively managed, using as investment universe, high-quality, short-maturity, dollar-denominated debt. The core of its portfolio is usually short-term investment grade corporate bonds (with A-BBB ratings), agency debt, US Treasury debt, and, where appropriate, floating-rate instruments or commercial paper. In order to maintain a low maturity of less than 12 months, the fund, as the securities held by it mature, reinvests the cash obtained from these maturities in new securities with equally short maturities, which allows the return received by the participant to be updated quickly.

In the aspect of generating returns, this is well understood if we disaggregate it into two levels. The first of these is the carry (the coupons that pay for the bonds held by the holding fund). As these are very short maturities and of high quality, most of the return comes from this periodic income, which is distributed on a regular monthly basis (the flow is seen and charged). The second level is the rise or fall in the price of securities when credit rates or spreads move. In this case, as the background has a short duration, the effect is not as noticeable as in other vehicles with a longer duration.

If interest rates rise, the fund’s NAV may be affected in the short term, but because the securities are constantly renewed, the fund will reinvest that cash in bonds with higher yields, increasing monthly income. Otherwise (lower rates) income will tend to fall, due to reinvestment at lower rates.

The main risk of this fund is not so much the direction of interest rates but rather the behavior of credit in the short term. This means that if the market demands more premium for lending to corporate issuers (spreads widening), the prices of those bonds adjust downwards and the ETF reflects this in its NAV. This risk is managed using high credit quality and diversification combinations between public and private debt. In widening situations, the fund will tend to rotate part of its portfolio to government securities, which better cushion the volatility of corporate credit.

Analysis - Macro Fundamental

In this section, we will analyse some macroeconomic indicators in detail to try to offer the reader an accurate perspective and a sound recommendation on the vehicle in question.

In the first proposed metric, we summarize in a synthesized way, in what amount is paying the short tranche of the curve and how exactly the portfolio of this ETF is being renewed. The chart’s main line (Combo A Core) combines four key elements to understand this (the level of the 3M (carry) tranche, the 3–6, 3–12, and 6–24-month slopes (the pickup that the ETF captures as it rolls to somewhat higher maturities), and recent monetary policy momentum).

The representation of the z-scores allows us to read it intuitively with respect to its own history, since we can appreciate how the different series are moving with respect to their historical mean. On the right-hand axis is the level of the “pure” (non-standardized) 3M tranche, which is the basis of the coupon that the ETF can distribute in the very short term. If this level drops or rises, VUSB revenue tends to adjust quickly because the portfolio expires in a staggered manner and reinvests the proceeds into new types.

At present, we can see that the environment fits in with a situation of falling interest rates, which is what is really happening. The 3M tranche remains relatively high by its historical standard, though it is no longer pushing up as before, while the 3-6 and 3-12-month micro-slopes remain neutral or negative, indicating that “stretching” the maturity slightly does not add much additional yield today. As a result, Combo A’s line has fallen from its 2022-2023 highs toward neutral areas. With this in hand, we can say that the carry is still interesting, but it is losing traction as the monetary policy momentum has changed.

This situation, translates into a very stable behavior of the fund's NAV and a gradual slippage of distributed profitability as the portfolio is renewing coupons at lower rates, all without abrupt changes as long as the process of cuts is orderly, as it seems that it is being and will be.

If the reader does not have access to this graph, he can replicate the idea by monitoring public indicators, which can be consulted in FRED (Federal Reserve database). The series to be monitored would then be the following: DGS3MO (3-month tranche level), DGS6MO and DGS1 (6-month and 1-year tranche levels, to calculate DGS6MO–DGS3MO and DGS1–DGS3MO), and DFF (official rates). To this, we can apply the following actionable rules:

  • If the DGS3MO series drops by 50–75 bp in two or three months, we can expect a slip in VUSB revenue in the next 2–4 months (the portfolio is being rolled over to lower rates).
  • If the slope DGS1–DGS3MO is maintained at a level greater than or equal to 20–30 bp for several weeks, there is a favorable situation for the carry, because of the roll they carry out.
  • In the case of Federal rates (DFF) are cut but the DGS3MO series is slow to go down, the carry holds something else (there is a transmission delay in the cut).

The second metric we propose measures the theoretical noise that can affect the NAV of the fund, even if it has a low duration. In this case, the line of Combo B (Combo B Core) is calculated based on the variables (inverted z-score, so that high values indicate positive signs) of:

  1. The spread between the Moody's Seasoned Corporate Bond (BAA) and the yield on the 10-year Treasury bond, which gives us a simple thermometer of the investment grade credit.
  2. The funding gap, calculated as the difference between the Fed Funds and the T-bill 3M, with which we detect frictions in the money markets.
  3. The NFCI of the Chicago Fed, which gives us a broad reading of the financial conditions that make up credit, existing.

As you can see in the graph, the levels that the reader sees on the right axis (spread Baa, funding gap and NFCI ) help us understand what is moving the signal when the composite goes up or down.

Currently, the Combo B Core component remains in a neutral-positive zone, which fits with relatively contained spreads, with a stress-free funding gap and a more or less loose NFCI. For VUSB, this means that the NAV risk from lending and microfinance is low, so the participant’s experience is very much like “cashing in coupons and little else,” with small, transient price variations when the market is turbulent.

The purpose of this second metric is to detect the stability of the fund price, looking at the credit and financing environments. The reader can follow in this case, these three public FRED indicators: BAA – DGS10 (look at BAA and DGS10 or the BAA10YM spread if you prefer), the funding gap = DFF – DGS3MO (series seen above), and the NFCI (Chicago Fed financial indicator). The practical signs in this case would be:

  • Positive environment: if the BAA–10Y is stable, the funding gap around 0.10%, and NFCI levels below zero, indicates comfortable conditions for the vehicle in question.
  • Precautionary signal: If the BAA–10Y rises 50 bp in a four- to eight-week stretch, the funding gap is greater than 0.25% on a sustained basis, or the NFCI goes to positive levels, conditions tighten. In that case, it makes sense to reduce positions and wait for metric readings to normalize.

Feature and Performance Analysis

In the previous captures, it can be observed the performance that the VUSB ETF has had, with respect to other ETFs of the same family, within the Vanguard manager and a clear comparison of costs, strategies, and assets under management. In this case we compare the VUSB ETF, against the VGUS and VBIL ETFs, which are also excellent vehicles to park liquidity. We can observe that, in the last 3 months, VUSB has performed the most, compared to the other two. This is mainly due to the fact that the fund analyzed here is actively managed, which makes its yield efficient, and invests almost all its capital in corporate bonds, which provides that extra yield compared to its peers (VBIL, VGUS), who invest all their portfolio in US government bonds.

On the expense side, we can see that VUSB has a somewhat higher expense ratio than the other two. A cost of 0.10% versus 0.07%. This extra cost (0.03%) exists to remunerate the active management of the VUSB fund, compared to the passive management and indexation proposed by the other two ETFs mentioned in this section.

Conclusion

My current view is that the vehicle should be molded. With Combo A (carry of the front of the curve and micro-slopes) already in the neutral zone, the income of the fund will tend to moderate as the portfolio is renewed, at somewhat lower coupons, while Combo B (credit and financing) remains at acceptable levels, with contained spreads and without tensions in money markets, which sustains a low NAV risk and a very stable price experience. In this context, the ETF performs well in its role as a cash generator to park cash with little volatility. I am not proposing to buy, because there is currently no clear catalyst to say that the fund’s income can improve in the short term. Therefore, if you have it in your portfolio, keep it, continue to fulfill its role as a cash generator, and do not buy it until we see a clear sign of appreciation of the slope of the 1 year and 3 month tranche. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Thermo Fisher Scientific: More Upside Likely As Headwinds Continue To Fade And Valuation Still Attractive
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last covered Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (NYSE:TMO)(NEOE:TMO:CA) in August with a buy rating, and the stock has performed nicely since then, gaining ~25% versus S&P 500’s (SPY) 6.4% gains. Moving forward, the stock has good revenue and margin growth prospects. The company’s revenues should benefit from fading uncertainty around drug pricing and tariffs, which impact its core pharma customers. In addition, reshoring of pharma manufacturing, recovering clinical research business, easing comps, and continued M&As should help revenues.

Margins should benefit from operating leverage, continuous improvement under the PPI business system, which now includes open AI integration, and synergy savings as the company integrates recent acquisitions. The stock is trading at a slight discount versus historical levels despite recovering trends, and I believe there is still a good upside left. Hence, I continue to rate the stock a buy.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

Thermo Fisher posted solid numbers in Q3 2025, with total revenue up about 5% year over year. Organic growth came in at 3%, M&A added roughly 1%, and foreign exchange contributed about 1%.

Looking at the segments, Life Sciences Solutions led the way with 8% revenue growth, including 5% organic, helped by strong performance in the bioproduction business and the research and safety market channel. Analytical Instruments was up 5%, with 4% organic growth, mainly driven by strength in electron microscopy and chromatography/mass spectrometry. Specialty Diagnostics delivered 4% growth, including 2% organic, supported by solid results in transplant diagnostics and immuno-diagnostics. The Laboratory Products and Biopharma Services segment grew 4% as well, with 3% organic, though that included about a 1% drag from the runoff of pandemic-related revenues.

Looking ahead, I am optimistic about Thermo Fisher's revenue growth prospects. Some of the headwinds that weighed on the company's growth over the last few quarters are starting to ease.

One of the major overhangs for big pharma companies, which are the major customers for Thermo Fisher, has been uncertainty around the U.S. drug pricing, as the Trump Administration called for significantly lower prices. This resulted in worries around the R&D budget of these pharma companies, which directly impacts TMO's sales. Tariffs also further complicated this situation.

However, there have been some signs of improvement of late. Earlier this month, Pfizer (PFE) signed a deal with the US government to sell drugs at lower prices through the TrumpRx portal and invest in the U.S. production in exchange for a reprieve in tariffs. This has improved the sentiments, and investors are expecting more such deals for other pharma companies, which should remove near-term uncertainty for the sector. This bodes well for Thermo Fisher's business.

The company is also poised to benefit as pharma and biotech customers expand their capacity in the U.S., aligning with the broader reshoring mandate. This should create incremental demand beyond day-to-day operations, especially for the company's bioproduction equipment and analytical instruments, and serve as a multi-year tailwind for the company's sales.

The company's Clinical Research business, which accounts for approximately one-fifth of its total revenue, is also poised to recover. Management pointed out that authorizations are up nicely and running well ahead of the current revenue levels. The sales in this business are also recovering and went from a mid-single-digit Y/Y decline in Q1 to flat in Q2 and up low single digits in Q3. I expect this acceleration to continue given strong authorization trends.

The company's other businesses, where the trends have been slow, are stabilizing as well. Academic and Government sales were down slightly Y/Y in Q3, but improved sequentially from Q2 as the funding visibility got a bit better compared to the uncertain environment earlier this year when the Trump Administration just started announcing the budget cuts related to universities.

China was soft, but trends are not getting incrementally worse, and given easier comparisons next year, it can move closer to flattish revenue performance next year. The runoff of pandemic-related revenue was another headwind and shaved one percentage point off 2025 growth, but this headwind will not repeat in 2026, helping growth acceleration.

Management has guided for a 3-6% organic growth target for the next couple of years. We are already at the low end of this range. Given the accelerating trends, I believe the company can easily achieve the mid to high end of this range in the coming years.

In addition to organic growth, the company should also benefit from bolt-on M&As. The recently acquired Solventum’s Filtration and Separation business (~$750 mn in annual revenue) expands Thermo Fisher's bioprocessing capabilities, while the purchase of Sanofi’s sterile fill-finish site in New Jersey strengthens its U.S. pharma services footprint. Even after these acquisitions, net leverage sits at ~2.9x EBITDA, which is reasonable and gives flexibility for more deals. So, I expect bolt-on M&As to continue complementing organic growth.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

Thermo Fisher posted strong margin gains in Q3 2025, with adjusted operating margin up 100 basis points year over year to 23.3%. The improvement was mainly driven by the company’s PPI (Practical Process Improvement) business system and tight cost controls, which delivered solid productivity gains that more than offset tariff pressures, FX headwinds, and some unfavorable mix. Adjusted gross margin came in at 41.9%, up 10 basis points from last year, while adjusted SG&A expenses as a percentage of revenue improved to 15.5%.

By segment, Life Sciences Solutions’ adjusted operating margin hit 37.4%, up 200 basis points. Specialty Diagnostics followed with a 150-basis-point gain to 27.4%, and Laboratory Products and Biopharma Services expanded 100 basis points to 14.5%. The only laggard was Analytical Instruments, where margins were down 230 basis points to 22.6%, mostly due to tariff and FX impacts. That said, it still marked a sequential improvement from Q2, which is encouraging.

Moving forward, I am optimistic about the company's margin growth prospects. With the revenue growth outlook positive, I believe the company should benefit from operating leverage due to increased sales.

The company is also doing a good job in terms of cost discipline and productivity improvements. The company's Practical Process Improvement (PPI) business system, which embeds a continuous improvement mindset, is helping the company. The company has also integrated OpenAI's enterprise tools into key operational areas like product development, service delivery, and customer engagement. This should further help improve execution, cycle times, and extract value from the company's deep data sets.

In addition, integration of the recent acquisitions (Solventum, Sanofi’s sterile fill-finish site) should also help margins as the Company starts realizing revenue synergies.

Management has guided for a 50-70 bps operating margin improvement in FY26 and FY27. Given the company already posted a 100 bps Y/Y improvement in margin last quarter, and conditions are improving from next year with revenue growth accelerating and the company comping tariff headwinds, I believe it is achievable.

Valuation and Rating

Thermo Fisher is trading at 25.19x FY25 consensus EPS estimates of $22.71 and 23.23x FY26 consensus EPS estimates of $24.62. Over the past five years, the stock has traded at an average forward P/E of ~23.9x. Since we are already in October, it makes sense to look at valuation on FY26 estimates, and on that basis, the stock is trading at a discount to historical levels.

I don't think the valuation discount relative to historical levels is justified. After several slow years, we are now seeing the early signs of re-acceleration in both revenue and profit growth. Given the potential for growth acceleration starting next year, I believe the stock should trade at a premium.

Thermo Fisher has a strong track record of execution both organically and through M&A, with a defensive revenue mix given its healthcare exposure and recurring revenue base (~83% revenue is recurring).

I believe it can easily re-rate to mid-to-high 20s as investors become comfortable with accelerating revenue growth prospects. This re-rating, combined with high single/low double-digit EPS growth potential, leaves good upside for the stock over the coming years. Hence, I continue to maintain my buy rating on the stock.

Risks

In addition to organic growth, Thermo Fisher pursues bolt-on mergers and acquisitions (M&A) to drive growth. Inorganic growth is relatively riskier compared to organic growth, and there are risks related to overpaying for an acquisition, integration challenges, and failure to realize expected synergies. Any of these outcomes could negatively impact the stock performance.

Thermo Fisher’s recent share price strength has also been partly driven by improved sentiment following Pfizer’s deal with the U.S. government under the TrumpRx initiative. Investors are anticipating that similar agreements may follow for other pharmaceutical companies, which would be beneficial for Thermo Fisher’s business. However, if such deals fail to materialize or if the momentum behind this trend slows, it could dampen customer sentiment and weigh on the stock’s valuation.

data.title: Zoomd Technologies: How A Microcap AdTech Player Is Winning The Open Internet Battle
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Zoomd Technologies Ltd. (OTCPK:ZMDTF, TSXV:ZOMD:CA) is a small yet highly profitable advertising technology firm that is currently in hypergrowth. Despite this, the firm trades at extremely attractive valuations, both on a P/E and FCF growth basis, with the valuations being supported by strong execution of management and sector tailwinds.

Since executing a strategic repositioning in Q2 of 2023, the firm has been able to see growth as high as the high double digits Y/Y, and has turned from unprofitability to net income margins as high as 31%. As repositioning efforts commenced, the company has diversified further between clients and sectors, continued to expand into new, high-margin sectors, and reduced operating expenses through the use of AI and machine learning. On top of this, the firm currently holds no GAAP-accountable debt, strengthening the risk/reward of this investment.

These factors, combined with recent catalysts of bullish analyst coverage and partnerships that expand the reach of Zoomd, all strengthen my bullish thesis in the company.

As part of my analysis of Zoomd Technologies Ltd., I would like to initiate coverage with a "Strong Buy" rating.

Business Overview

Zoomd Technologies is a global digital advertising company specializing in the acquisition of mobile users through marketing on the open internet. The company fundamentally differentiates itself by making its performance the critical metric behind its business: the company only gets paid when clients acquire paying customers, instead of just clicks or impressions.

The company operates in what is called the "open internet", as opposed to the typical "walled garden" approach seen by companies such as Google, Meta, and TikTok.

Major platforms operating within the "walled garden" are effective in acquiring customers not only due to the massive amount of reach available but also due to the immense amount of user data these companies can use to better target their ads.

However, this comes at the expense of several factors. Advertisers often struggle with limited transparency and control, and increased crowding has led to costs significantly increasing, forcing advertisers to diversify into options outside these "walled gardens".

To be able to capture this huge chunk of advertiser budget split, Zoomd uses AI-driven optimizations as well as machine learning to understand how advertising can be best suited for each product and each geography, and displays the real-time information to the client through a single, unified dashboard.

Explosive Top And Bottom Line Growth

Zoomd has experienced tremendous growth in its top line. When observing the quarterly revenue numbers from the company's investor slide deck, as shown above, the company has shown 40% growth Y/Y, or 86% when looking at the TTM revenue compared to TTM revenue 1 year ago.

This explosive growth can be attributed to several factors. However, the most important one was due to the repositioning efforts announced by the firm in Q2 '23. The repositioning efforts aimed to diversify their clientele away from single industries, and into more sectors and geographies. Additionally, the firm massively slashed operating costs through headcount adjustments and the discontinuation of several low-potential business lines, such as their operation in the crypto space, which was shown to be a low-margin side of the business.

As previously reported, the cost reductions, which extend beyond headcount adjustments, are projected to result in annual savings of approximately $4 million. In addition to these cost savings, we have announced the discontinuation of several non-profitable and low-potential business lines. This strategic decision allows us to reallocate efforts and resources towards Zoomd’s core business. - Amit Bohemsky, Chairman of Zoomd

The repositioning efforts of the at-the-time newly appointed CEO, Ido Almany, who was formerly the CEO of American Express’ licensee in Israel, were a grand success, transitioning the firm from struggling to stay afloat, to one that maintains high, growing margins and carries strong fundamental trends.

These efforts are further observed through the operating costs as a percentage of revenue. The firm consistently has shown significant improvements in operational costs, driven by the implementation of AI and machine learning and headcount cuts, driving operational costs down to 16% from a high of 48% in Q1 '23. Such a strong improvement signals strong operating leverage in the business, driving net income margin to as high as 31% as of the last quarter, from the lows of deep unprofitability.

Extreme Undervaluation

Despite such explosive growth, strong positive rhetoric from management, and a clean balance sheet (no GAAP-accountable debt obligations, and 7.7M of cash net of all obligations), mentions of buyback programs and even M&A, the company still trades at ~10x P/E on a TTM basis, as seen on Seeking Alpha as of October 22nd.

Reverse DCF Model

From time to time, I like to include a reverse DCF model in my analysis. Personally, I do not put much faith into the projections of future revenues, incomes, and FCF unless the figures come from management themselves. In the case of Zoomd, while we have solid rhetoric from management, no formal guidance was disclosed. Therefore, I do not want to speculate on a potential growth rate for such a company. However, I can run a Reverse Discounted Cash Flows (RDCF) model to determine what kind of FCF growth the market is currently pricing in.

Some analysis assumptions:

  • WACC of 12.28% (based on a high beta and no cost of debt, due to 0 GAAP accountable obligations)
  • Perpetual Growth Rate: 3% (After 9 years of cash flows, highly conservative assumption)

There are many uncertainties in such an analysis, and it should not hold any ground as an investment thesis on its own. However, it does provide some color as a very high level analysis around what the market is currently pricing into this stock.

As we can see, an average FCF growth of ~20% over the next 9 years is incredibly minuscule, considering how quickly the company has grown in the last year, with over 200% FCF growth. This is further amplified by the fact that the company is functioning in a rapidly growing market, as highlighted by Zoomd themselves.

Bullish Analyst Coverage

Zoomd has very recently (October 22nd, 2025) received its first-ever analyst rating. The rating, from ATB Capital Markets, put an "Outperform" rating on the stock, with a price target of C$4.00 (currently ~65% upside, as of close on Oct. 22nd), highlighting the firm's scalability, profitable growth, and potential for valuation multiple expansion.

In-app consumer spending is ~$200bn annually, and acquiring new users is the lifeblood of these businesses. New users for entertainment, e-commerce, and online gambling are high-value. These clients are willing to pay attractive rates per user.

Clients pay for new user acquisition and other high-value KPIs. This results in high ROI, alongside advertising/marketing performance that is directly tied to client revenue and has identifiable return.

Favorable Catalysts

Continuing with the first point of the ATM Capital Markets analyst, new users for entertainment, e-commerce, and online gambling are considered high-value. This statement from the analyst could not have been timed better, as the firm has recently announced, through a press release, a strategic partnership with E2. E2-Quadrat is a global leader in digital marketing and technology solutions for the sports and betting industry.

This strategic partnership shows management's focus on continuing to expand their reach, specifically into sectors where advertising rates may command a premium.

This partnership reflects Zoomd’s strategy of aligning with category leaders in high-growth verticals. This recent expansion into new segments and partnerships reflects the company’s ongoing efforts to diversify its customer concentration - Amit Bohemsky, Chairman of Zoomd

Risks

Concentration Risk

While I believe the fundamentals of the business remain strong, as with any investment, there are always risks present. One risk that is often brought up with Zoomd is around customer concentration, specifically around their largest customer, which is speculated to be Shein.

While Zoomd has historically had significant issues with concentration, specifically in the realm of crypto, it now represents less than 10% of their clientele size.

However, the risk of concentration still persists within their portfolio, this time due to only 5 clients making up the majority of the revenue. During the Q1 '25 earnings call, investors were able to get the following information:

As we reported, 85% of our revenues come from 5 largest customers.

While this definitely will raise alarm bells for investors, it is also important to realize that each of these customers operates within different countries and even different continents, each with different KPIs and targets for each region. As reported by the CEO on the same earnings call, one of the top clients (speculated to be Shein) operates within more than 30 different countries. This signals continued trust in Zoomd to deliver on their KPIs, and that Shein has, over time, expanded its partnership with the firm due to steady and successful results.

This is further reinforced by the strong client retention rates that Zoomd has shown.
