Business Model Erosion to Competition

Another concern mentioned by Zoomd investors centers around the rapid growth seen within the industry, specifically with peers such as APP (AppLovin).

In the case of AppLovin, the company operates in a fundamentally different way than Zoomd. First, AppLovin does not function within the open internet space in the same way that Zoomd does. AppLovin uses an AI-only approach to create ads only within the mobile apps using AppLovin's proprietary software. This is a stark difference from Zoomd, whose ads may appear in places spanning between websites, social platforms, or even influencers. In this sense, Zoomd can be considered a more traditional open internet operator.

Additionally, it is worth noting that due to the much broader potential reach of open internet advertising, as opposed to the less wide-spanning approach that APP has taken, Zoomd is better suited to tackle the wide variability within the fragmented open internet, which may require an approach that requires human intervention and industry knowledge/expertise, such as in the case of influencer campaigns.

Thus, while erosion of the business model is a possibility, at least in the near future, the two companies are not direct competitors, and it may take years before both companies mature to a point where they are competing for market share on a scale that directly impacts their top line.

Conclusion

Zoomd Technologies presents itself as a highly attractive micro-cap pick. The company has shown strong growth in not only the top line, but has also shown immense margin expansion, led by effective cost-cutting initiatives. Even with such growth, the firm trades at only 10x TTM P/E.

Despite being a micro-cap, which is typically associated with high risk, the firm has a clean balance sheet, and a strong management team that has pulled the company out of a dire situation, and has turned the situation into not only profitability, but even hyper-growth.

All this information, topped with strong sentiment from the recent analyst coverage, as well as management mentions of potential for stock buybacks and M&A activities, signals that the company has a strong footing over their core business and plans to reward shareholders with the fruits of their business.

I am initiating a "Strong Buy" rating on ZOMD:CA (or ZMDTF on the OTC), due to strong fundamentals and market demand, execution strength of management, and deep mispricing by the market.

data.title: Strategy: Finally Time To Buy Again (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

MicroStrategy (NASDAQ:MSTR) has been sliding down for the last 3 months.

But after the recent crypto flush out, and given the nice bounce from the recent lows, I believe there’s a good setup for MSTR and potentially Bitcoin (BTC-USD) going forward.

MSTR’s dip looks buyable, both in terms of technicals and fundamentals.

A Quick Recap

My last MSTR post was almost six months ago.

Back then, I made the case that MSTR was reaching a buyable breakout. While the stock did go on to rally over the next month or so, we have been in a steady slide since mid-July.

More recently, I spoke about Bitcoin looking toppy, but with room for a final rally into the $150,00 zone.

Following the latest flush out in crypto, which saw $20 billion in capital wiped out, I believe we now have the right set-up in Bitcoin and Strategy to see a meaningful bounce, perhaps even new all-time highs.

The Fundamentals

The fundamentals still look supportive of higher Bitcoin prices. The “debasement trade”, which I covered recently in this post, is very much still in play.

The current administration wants to outgrow the deficit and do so with the help of a dovish Fed.

With inflation data coming out today weaker than expected, the road may now be clear for more cuts, and Powell has even talked about ending QT in the near future.

Plus, as I highlighted in my last gold article, the spectacular rally we have seen in gold could soon be followed by a similar rally in Bitcoin, if history is to repeat itself.

All this at a time when Crypto sentiment is still quite bearish. This is when you buy, not when you sell.

All in all, there’s a good confluence of macro tailwinds, bearish sentiment, and positioning that tells me we could be in for another fast and furious rally.

The Technicals

From a technical perspective, this seems like a good risk/reward setup.

Let’s start by looking at the underlying asset, Bitcoin.

For the time being, Bitcoin has bounced beautifully off the 200-day EMA, for now, and this could set the low.

Ideally, I would love to see a quick double bottom form, much like we saw back in March, with a quick dip below the 200 EMA, finding support at $97K and potentially forming a larger bullish divergence with the RSI.

Now, let’s look at MSTR.

Like Bitcoin, MSTR looks like it could be close to a bottom. The RSI has trended higher over the last few weeks, though arguably we are still below the key EMAs, so the chart looks a bit worse.

Zooming out to the weekly, we can see that MSTR is within an accumulation range that has been going on for months.

We saw this happen back in March 2024 before the big rally, and this could be a repeat.

Like Bitcoin, there’s room for a slight retest of lower lows, but either way, this price today seems quite good if we are indeed expecting a significant rally.

A simple retest of recent highs would imply a 60% rally from today’s price, which is nothing to scoff at.

MSTR over Bitcoin?

I would defend owning both, but I would expect MSTR to outperform Bitcoin if it rallies to new highs.

The current NAV premium for MSTR is historically low, close to 1.2, and I’d expect this to increase as Bitcoin rallies.

Why?

Firstly, because MSTR already has Bitcoin in its balance sheet, this increases its leverage potential.

The whole strategy behind Strategy works when Bitcoin is rising, and that is a reason there’s a premium.

On the other hand, FOMO and sentiment should also make retail names in crypto catch a bid.

With short interest near 10%, this also gives MSTR some short squeeze potential.

Risks

There’s also the chance that the Bitcoin cycle has ended, and we don’t actually see new highs for some time. This is a distinct possibility investors should not ignore.

We are nearing what would be the end of the Bitcoin 4-year cycle, and we are near important levels of support after the recent sell-off.

If Bitcoin breaks below $90K, this would be a big hit to my bull thesis.

Final Thoughts

All in all, I like the setup right now for MSTR and Bitcoin. This could be risky, of course, but there’s plenty of upside potential. If Bitcoin breaks towards new all-time highs, there’s room for MSTR to even double from here.

data.title: Gold Hits A Speedbump
data.description:
It has been a strange year in so many ways, but one of this year’s odder bedfellows has been the tandem duo of US stocks and gold. These two asset classes don’t normally tango together.

In 1979, gold was just about the only thing that was working for portfolios, while the S&P 500 was in the dumps and bond markets were roiled by the Volcker shock of ultra-tight monetary policy.

Two decades later, 1999 was a go-go year for any stock with even the flimsiest connection to the Internet, while gold had nothing to offer other than being pretty to look at.

This year, the precious metal and the blue-chip stock index have been at the forefront of a giddy general melt-up for financial assets. Gold, in particular, has been on a tear since late summer, shooting up by more than 30 percent from the middle of August to the end of last week.

But this week, suddenly, things went south and the price of gold dropped by nearly seven percent in just two days, no doubt to the chagrin of those coming late to the party.

To Hedge or Not to Hedge

The timing of this pullback was a little odd. As we wrote about in our column last week, all the talk of the town in recent days has been about a stock market bubble. Bubble talk, in general, gets people to start thinking about hedging.

Gold, of course, has long been thought of as a go-to hedge asset – witness that start turn during the stagflation miasma of the late 1970s. One might have thought, rationally, that this week would kick off with a big pullback in stocks while gold rose some more.

But rationality has precious little to do with the ways of the markets in these times of ours. Stocks have continued blithely to ignore anyone waving wildly at stratospheric valuation levels, surging to yet another record high by the end of the week.

It is entirely possible, of course, that gold’s performance this year doesn’t actually have all that much to do with hedging. There are other, potentially cheaper ways to offset exposure to US stocks if one is so inclined, including simple S&P 500 put options.

Capital markets are starting to look a lot more like prop bets than anything else, where you can take a position for or against just about any movement large or small in just about any corner of the globe using an ever-expanding arsenal of easy-to-obtain assets.

Maybe there’s nothing more to the post-August rally in gold than the simple fact of all that liquidity coursing around the world has to go somewhere, so a good chunk of it might as well go to something that has been around for thousands of years.

Just a Pretty Hunk of Meta

The other thing to remember about gold is that it has no other source of value than price appreciation. No interest coupons, no dividend payments, nothing other than a belief that the price will be higher tomorrow than it is today.

Yes, it is a scarce asset with a finite supply, and over long periods of time the scarcity factor delivers value. But not always. Gold rose, for understandable reasons, during and after the stock market crash in 2008, reaching a then-peak in 2011.

But prices fell after that, and didn’t regain that 2011 peak price until the middle of 2020. In the post-pandemic period through 2023 gold’s performance was mostly flat. It was no help to investors looking for a hedge during the equity bear market of 2022, with prices falling around 15 percent from April to October of that year (the sharp rise of interest rates during this period made gold, as a non-interest bearing asset, less attractive).

What happens next remains to be seen, of course. Today, stocks are up, gold is down and a belated inflation report (delayed due to the government shutdown) delivered no surprises versus expectations. In the wake of that powerful surge in gold prices from August to last week, though, a more protracted pullback would not be out of the question.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CEF Weekly Review: CLO Equity Continues To Struggle
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund [CEF] market activity from both the bottom-up – highlighting individual fund news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the third week of October. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company [BDC] as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

It was an unusual week for the CEF market, as nearly all NAVs were higher and yet nearly all discounts were wider. Typically, discounts move in the same direction as NAVs (in price terms, i.e. discounts tend to tighten when NAVs move higher and vice-versa). This tells us that despite fairly strong markets, there is some anxiety on the part of CEF investors. We see a similar, if less pronounced, pattern on the month-to-date chart as well.

CEF sector discounts have widened back to their historic average level.

Market Themes

The CLO Equity CEF sector continues to struggle, with NAVs down again in September, with the average down around 3%. Least affected, as usual, are the partial-CLO equity funds like XFLT and EIC.

Year-to-date, NAVs are down close to 20%.

In Q3, the average fall was around 6%, which is quite a bit more than the NII generated by the funds. The story for NAV drops this year has been the tightening in credit spreads, and it’s true that spreads were tighter through Q3, though not by much. Anticipation of lower short-term rates, which accelerated in September on the back of Powell’s dovish pivot at the end of September, has been another driver.

Perhaps credit worries are affecting valuations as well. First Brands was not a big exposure in the overall CLO sector. On average it appears to be around 0.24% of total CLO assets. The implicit ~10x leverage means CLO Equity exposure could be north of 2%.

Market Commentary

The Pioneer loan CEF PHD announced its liquidating distribution of $9.738549. As was the case with HNW and PHT, this is quite a bit below the NAV of $10.12. The press release said once again that there might be additional distributions to come, which would make sense. Bank loans are not the most liquid assets, and the fund is likely shopping around for a better price on the rump portfolio. The price did not react much, which suggests the market expects to be made whole.

The FS Credit Opportunities Corp (FSCO) - a fund allocated in large part to private loans - was a big mover earlier in the week. There was nothing obvious here except, perhaps, a highlight of its First Brands exposure. This wasn’t exactly a secret. The fund has held a position for at least a year. Interestingly, it appears to have increased it from $34m to $54m most recently, which worked out to around 3.5% of the NAV. If fully written down, the loss would be a bit above its quarterly distribution - in effect, a loss of 4 months of income.

The fact that the fund held a position in what looks to be a house of cards looks bad not just in terms of the NAV loss but also in terms of what it implies about the rest of its portfolio. The fact that it increased its position shortly before the blow-up suggests an absence of the level of risk aversion evident in nearly all other public BDC/BDC-like entities. We continue to favor BDCs over CEFs with private loan exposure, such as FSCO.
